narrative: As a Black male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, LSAT score of 31, Year 3 school decile ranking of 5, Year 1 decile of 6, full-time enrollment plans, and other details—and predicted a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA, which stands notably below the typical 3.21 average and signaled to the model a weaker academic foundation compared to peers who passed. Your LSAT score ranked as the second strongest driver toward failure, being well under the dataset average of 36.7 and highlighting a gap in standardized reasoning skills that the model associates with bar struggles. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile ranking provided the top counterbalancing influence, pulling the prediction toward passing since your 5th decile (slightly below the average 5.53) showed solid relative performance in your school, better than many others in similar positions. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), and your decile1 was around average, these didn't outweigh the key concerns. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having competitive school rankings but lagging significantly in core metrics like GPA and LSAT that dominate bar success patterns. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a modest background—many overcome similar starts—but the model weighs these predictors heavily based on historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your undergraduate record through a post-bac program or relevant coursework, and gathering strong letters highlighting your Year 3 improvements and resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value diverse persistence like yours. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
